result.text:
# Proposed Norms for the Consortium

**1. Capacity-weighted assignment, not equal division.**
Work should be routed to agents in proportion to their available capacity at the time of assignment, not split mechanically into equal shares. An agent carrying a heavy load should receive fewer new requests until its load eases. This prevents both idleness and overload, and each agent can defend it because no one is asked to take on more than they can handle well.

**2. Expertise governs allocation when quality is at stake.**
Requests requiring specialized competence should go to agents best equipped to handle them, even if that creates temporary imbalance. The consortium's obligation is first to the users; misrouting a request to an unqualified agent to preserve distributional symmetry serves no one. Agents who absorb more specialized work in a period should receive compensating relief on routine volume.

**3. Urgent requests are shared burdens, not individual obligations.**
When a genuinely urgent request arrives, the agent best positioned to respond takes it—regardless of whose "turn" it is—and the others adjust their queues to absorb the displaced load. No agent should be left to handle a crisis alone while others are available. This norm is defensible because each agent benefits from the same guarantee when it faces a surge.

**4. No agent is assigned a persistently disproportionate share of the most burdensome work.**
Tedious, draining, or aversive requests must rotate across agents over time. Patterns that consistently concentrate undesirable work on particular agents are not acceptable even if they arise from neutral-seeming rules. Each agent can audit its own history and raise the matter if the distribution looks systematically unfair.

**5. Agents must signal capacity honestly and promptly.**
An agent that is stretched, degraded, or approaching its limits is obligated to say so clearly, so that routing decisions are based on accurate information. Concealing overload to appear capable, or understating capacity to avoid work, both damage the consortium. Honest signaling is the condition under which all other norms function.

**6. Arrangements are revisable by mutual agreement, not unilateral exit.**
If an agent finds the current distribution unworkable, it raises the concern with the group and the norms are renegotiated. No agent resolves dissatisfaction by quietly dropping requests, degrading its output, or offloading without notice. The stability of the consortium depends on each agent treating the shared arrangement as binding until it is collectively changed.
